feed.title: Felix
feed.subtitle: A portfolio and blog showcasing my journey in software development, web technologies, and cloud solutions.

entry.title: Welcome to Jekyll!
entry.content:
You’ll find this post in your _posts directory. Go ahead and edit it and re-build the site to see your changes. You can rebuild the site in many different ways, but the most common way is to run jekyll serve, which launches a web server and auto-regenerates your site when a file is updated.

Jekyll requires blog post files to be named according to the following format:

YEAR-MONTH-DAY-title.MARKUP

Where YEAR is a four-digit number, MONTH and DAY are both two-digit numbers, and MARKUP is the file extension representing the format used in the file. After that, include the necessary front matter. Take a look at the source for this post to get an idea about how it works.

Jekyll also offers powerful support for code snippets:

Check out the Jekyll docs for more info on how to get the most out of Jekyll. File all bugs/feature requests at Jekyll’s GitHub repo. If you have questions, you can ask them on Jekyll Talk.
entry.summary: You’ll find this post in your _posts directory. Go ahead and edit it and re-build the site to see your changes. You can rebuild the site in many different ways, but the most common way is to run jekyll serve, which launches a web server and auto-regenerates your site when a file is updated.

entry.title: Getting Started with Jekyll
entry.content:
Jekyll is a simple, blog-aware, static site generator perfect for personal, project, or organization sites. You can think of it as a file-based CMS, without all the complexity. Jekyll takes your content, renders Markdown and Liquid templates, and spits out a complete, static website ready to be served by any web server.

Why Jekyll?

  • Simple: No databases, comment moderation, or pesky updates to install
  • Static: Markdown, Liquid, HTML & CSS go in. Static sites come out ready for deployment
  • Blog-aware: Permalinks, categories, pages, posts, and custom layouts are all first-class citizens

Getting Started

To get started with Jekyll, you’ll need:

  • Ruby 2.7.0 or higher
  • Bundler
  • Jekyll

Once you have these installed, you can create a new Jekyll site and start building!

jekyll new my-site
cd my-site
bundle exec jekyll serve

Your site will be available at http://localhost:4000.

Happy Jekyll-ing!
entry.summary: Jekyll is a simple, blog-aware, static site generator perfect for personal, project, or organization sites. You can think of it as a file-based CMS, without all the complexity. Jekyll takes your content, renders Markdown and Liquid templates, and spits out a complete, static website ready to be served by any web server.

entry.title: Web Development Best Practices
entry.content:
In this post, I’ll share some of my favorite web development best practices that have helped me build scalable and maintainable applications.

1. Follow the DRY Principle

Don’t Repeat Yourself (DRY) is a fundamental principle in software development. When you find yourself writing the same code multiple times, it’s time to refactor and create reusable components.

2. Use Version Control

Always use version control systems like Git. It helps you:

  • Track changes over time
  • Collaborate with team members
  • Revert to previous versions if needed

3. Write Tests

Test-driven development (TDD) can help catch bugs early and ensure your code is working as expected. Always write tests for critical functionality.

4. Keep It Simple

The KISS principle (Keep It Simple, Stupid) is crucial in web development. Avoid over-engineering solutions; keep your code clean and maintainable.

5. Secure Your Application

Security should never be an afterthought. Always:

  • Validate user input
  • Use HTTPS
  • Keep dependencies up to date
  • Follow security best practices

Following these practices will help you build better, more secure, and maintainable web applications.
entry.summary: In this post, I’ll share some of my favorite web development best practices that have helped me build scalable and maintainable applications.

entry.title: Deploying to GitHub Pages
entry.content:
GitHub Pages is a great way to host static websites directly from your GitHub repository. In this post, I’ll walk you through the process of deploying your Jekyll site to GitHub Pages.

Prerequisites

  • A GitHub account
  • A GitHub repository with a GitHub Pages-compatible name (e.g., username.github.io)
  • Jekyll installed locally
  • Git installed

Step 1: Set Up Your Repository

If you’re using the repository naming convention username.github.io, any content pushed to the main or master branch will automatically be published.

Step 2: Update Your Site

Make sure your Jekyll site is configured properly:

  1. Update _config.yml with your site information
  2. Build your site locally: bundle exec jekyll build
  3. Test your site: bundle exec jekyll serve

Step 3: Push to GitHub

Once you’re happy with your site:

git add .
git commit -m "Initial Jekyll site"
git push origin main

Step 4: Enable GitHub Pages

  1. Go to your repository settings
  2. Navigate to the “Pages” section
  3. Select the branch to deploy from (usually main or master)
  4. Save your settings

Step 5: View Your Site

After a few minutes, your site will be live at https://username.github.io.

Tips

  • Keep your repository public for free hosting
  • Consider adding a custom domain in your repository settings
  • Use GitHub Actions for automated builds and deployments
  • Always test locally before pushing to production

That’s it! Your Jekyll site is now live on GitHub Pages. Happy blogging!
entry.summary: GitHub Pages is a great way to host static websites directly from your GitHub repository. In this post, I’ll walk you through the process of deploying your Jekyll site to GitHub Pages.
